behaviors.justification: The HMAC-SHA256 check is deterministic crypto that can be tested by mocking the signature verification function and verifying the handler returns 401. Request body parsing could introduce edge cases, but the core behavior is testable at unit level.
behaviors.test_description: Send a webhook request with an X-Signature-256 header that does NOT match HMAC-SHA256(body). Verify the handler returns 401 status code. Verify the event is not forwarded to the queue (queue.enqueue is not called).

behaviors.description: Event persisted to queue before HTTP 200 response
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the HTTP response is sent before the queue write completes, causing data loss. This cannot be tested with pure mocks; it requires verifying actual async sequencing with a real test queue or async mock that tracks timing.
behaviors.test_description: Execute a valid webhook request against the handler. Verify that the event persists in the queue before or simultaneously with the HTTP 200 response. Use a real or async test queue; verify the enqueue operation completes before the response status is sent to the client.

behaviors.description: Handler returns 200 without blocking on downstream processing
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is the HTTP response getting delayed or blocked by downstream processing. This requires running actual async code and measuring response timing, which is integration-level behavior, not a unit-level logic check.
behaviors.test_description: Send a webhook request where downstream processing is configured to take > 1 second (via mock with artificial delay). Verify the HTTP 200 response is returned within 100-500ms. Verify that downstream processing continues asynchronously after the response is returned.

behaviors.description: Response latency stays under 3 seconds under concurrent load
behaviors.justification: Wall-clock response time under concurrent load is not testable at unit level. This requires running multiple concurrent requests with realistic downstream latencies and measuring actual end-to-end timing.
behaviors.test_description: Concurrently send 10+ webhook requests to the handler. Configure downstream processing to add 0.5–2 second delays (via mock delays). Measure wall-clock response time for each request from arrival to HTTP 200. Verify all responses complete within 3 seconds, even when earlier events are still being processed.

behaviors.description: Duplicate events (same ID) are not enqueued twice
behaviors.justification: The duplicate check likely relies on a persistent store (database, Redis, etc.). A Unit test with only in-memory mock would not catch failures in the actual persistence mechanism. Integration level with a real or test database is required.
behaviors.test_description: Send the same webhook event (same ID) twice. Verify the event is enqueued exactly once, not twice. The test must use a real test database or persistent store for duplicate checking, not an in-memory mock.

behaviors.description: Handler returns 503 with Retry-After when queue is full
behaviors.justification: This is testing error-handling logic in the handler. A mock queue that throws a 'full' error allows unit-level testing of the handler's response. The risk is low that the real queue's error differs significantly from the mock.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the queue client mock to throw an error indicating the queue is full. Send a webhook request to the handler. Verify the handler returns 503 status code and includes a Retry-After header with a reasonable retry delay (e.g., 60 seconds).

behaviors.description: Queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit
behaviors.justification: This tests the queue's own behavior, not the handler logic. A mock queue would not verify the actual enforcement. A real queue instance with actual capacity limits is required to verify the behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real or test queue instance with a configured capacity limit (e.g., max 10 events), fill it to capacity. Attempt to enqueue one additional event. Verify that the queue rejects the over-capacity event (raises an error or reports queue full). Verify using the actual queue implementation, not a mock.
